content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Artificiality is contagious.Modern education has been steadily shifting in a direction that is not immediately visible to the people inside it. On the surface, nothing appears to have fundamentally changed: students still learn, read, watch explanations, and complete tasks. But beneath this surface, something more subtle is happening. The act of thinking itself is slowly being redistributed outside the human mind.
We usually describe technology as a tool for access. It gives us faster information, easier explanations, and more efficient ways to solve problems. But this framing misses a deeper transformation. Technology does not only change how we access knowledge - it changes where knowledge is processed.

What used to happen inside the mind is increasingly happening outside of it.
Memory is no longer primarily a system of retention. It has become a system of retrieval. We no longer rely on remembering information, but on knowing where to find it.
Search engines replaced recall. Video replaced structured reasoning. Algorithms replaced selection. And now, AI systems increasingly replace the formation of answers themselves.
At first glance, this looks like progress. It reduces effort, removes friction, and speeds up learning. But cognitive systems do not evolve without consequences. When one function becomes externalized, the internal system that once supported it begins to weaken or reorganize.

This shift is not dramatic. It does not happen through obvious loss. Nothing disappears overnight. Instead, functions gradually detach from the mind and relocate into external systems that are always available, always faster, and often more reliable than internal processing.
As a result, thinking becomes less of a continuous internal process and more of an interaction with external tools.
You do not reconstruct knowledge anymore - you retrieve it.
You do not develop answers - you select them.
You do not build understanding step by step - you assemble it from outputs produced elsewhere.

The important change is not that humans stop thinking. They still do.
The change is that thinking is no longer contained within a single cognitive loop. It is split across systems.
Part of it happens in memory. Part of it in search. Part of it in recommendation systems. Part of it in language models that generate structured responses in real time.
What was once a unified internal process becomes a distributed network of cognitive functions.

This creates a new kind of dependency that is not immediately obvious. It is not dependence on a single tool, but dependence on an external structure of thinking itself. The mind no longer carries the full burden of cognition, but it also no longer fully owns the process.
And this is where the shift becomes important.
Because once thinking is externalized, the boundary between “knowing” and “accessing knowledge” begins to blur. The ability to retrieve information starts to replace the ability to form it internally. And over time, this changes how understanding itself is experienced.

We begin to mistake access for comprehension.
We begin to confuse responsiveness with reasoning.
And slowly, the mind becomes less of a place where thoughts are formed, and more of a control interface for systems that think on its behalf.

This is not a collapse of cognition. It is a reconfiguration of it. But it is a reconfiguration in which the center of gravity moves outward.
And once that shift happens, it is no longer clear where thinking actually resides.
